When people bring Jesus news of tragedy—Galileans slaughtered while making offerings, eighteen crushed beneath a collapsing tower—they want Him to explain it. They expect Him to rank sinners, to tell them whose sins were greatest, to solve the problem of suffering with moral arithmetic. But Jesus refuses this entirely. He doesn't debate whether the dead were worse than others. He interrupts the entire conversation with an urgent demand: repent, or you will perish too. This is the only response Jesus offers, and He repeats it twice in succession. There is something almost violent in His refusal to let tragedy become an intellectual puzzle. People die senselessly. That is not the point. The point is that you are still alive, and you are not ready. The fig tree parable that follows makes this concrete—a tree given three years to bear fruit, given one more year with extra care, but destined for the axe if it remains fruitless. Jesus is not speaking in abstractions. He is saying that the time for bearing spiritual fruit is limited, that patience has boundaries, that judgment is real and approaching.

Yet immediately Jesus demonstrates what spiritual fruit actually looks like. On the Sabbath, He heals a woman bent over for eighteen years, and in that single act, He exposes the moral bankruptcy of those who protest His work. The synagogue leader cares more about law-keeping than liberation. Jesus asks the devastating question: if you will untie an animal for water on the Sabbath, will you not loose a daughter of Abraham from Satan's chains? The woman's healing is not a breach of the Sabbath—it is the Sabbath's true purpose finally being lived out. She stands straight and glorifies God. This is what fruit looks like: a person made whole, a life returned to worship.

Then Jesus speaks of the kingdom in images of growth: a mustard seed becoming a tree, yeast permeating dough. But He does not mean that the kingdom is inevitably expanding toward everyone. When asked directly whether only a few will be saved, He answers by intensifying the demand to enter through the narrow door. Many will try and fail. Many will have eaten and drunk with Him, heard Him teach in their streets, yet find themselves outside, excluded, facing weeping and gnashing of teeth. The kingdom grows, yes—but many who assume they belong will discover they do not. Meanwhile, people from every direction will come to recline at the kingdom's table. The criterion is not familiarity with Jesus or proximity to His teaching. It is something else entirely—something known only to the Master of the house.

Finally, Jesus turns toward Jerusalem, indifferent to Herod's threat, steady in His purpose. He calls the king a fox—cunning, small, insignificant compared to what He must do. He laments over the city He is traveling toward, a Jerusalem that kills prophets, that stones messengers, that refuses to be gathered under His wings. His rejection is not accidental or surprising. It is written into the very pattern of Israel's history. Yet even in this moment of lament, there is a strange hope: one day, when they say "Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord," they will see Him again. This is not a promise of universal salvation. It is a promise that His vindication is certain, and Jerusalem's recognition of it—however delayed—is part of God's unstoppable plan. The question Jesus leaves with His listeners, and with us, is not abstract. It is personal, urgent, and unavoidable: Will you repent? Will you bear fruit? Will you truly enter through the narrow door?
